The **romanization of Arabic** is the systematic rendering of [written](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic") and [spoken](/wiki/Varieties_of_Arabic "Varieties of Arabic") [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") in the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"). Romanized Arabic is used for various purposes, among them transcription of names and titles, [cataloging](/wiki/Cataloging "Cataloging") Arabic language works, [language education](/wiki/Language_education "Language education") when used instead of or alongside the Arabic script, and representation of the language in scientific publications by [linguists](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"). These formal systems, which often make use of [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritics "Diacritics") and non-standard Latin characters, are used in academic settings for the benefit of non-speakers, contrasting with informal means of written communication used by speakers such as the Latin-based [Arabic chat alphabet](/wiki/Arabic_chat_alphabet "Arabic chat alphabet").

Different systems and strategies have been developed to address the inherent problems of rendering various Arabic varieties in the Latin script. Examples of such problems are the symbols for Arabic [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") that do not exist in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") or other [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") languages; the means of representing the [Arabic definite article](/wiki/Arabic_definite_article "Arabic definite article"), which is always spelled the same way in written Arabic but has numerous pronunciations in the spoken language depending on context; and the representation of short vowels (usually *i u* or *e o*, accounting for variations such as *[Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") and Moslem* or *[Mohammed](/wiki/Mohammed "Mohammed"), Muhammad and Mohamed*).

## Method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Method")]

Romanization is often termed "transliteration", but this is not technically correct.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") is the direct representation of foreign *letters* using Latin symbols, while most systems for romanizing Arabic are actually [transcription](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") systems, which represent the *sound* of the language, since short vowels and geminate consonants, for example, do not usually appear in Arabic writing. As an example, the following rendering “**munāẓaratu l-ḥurūfi l-ʻarabīyah**” of [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): مناظرة الحروف العربية is a transcription, indicating the pronunciation; an example of transliteration would be **mnaẓrḧ alḥrwf alʻrbyḧ**.

## Romanization standards and systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Romanization standards and systems")]

Principal standards and systems are:

### Early Romanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early Romanization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/1506_Arabic_transcription_-_Arte_para_ligeramente_saber_la_lengua_arauiga.jpg/250px-1506_Arabic_transcription_-_Arte_para_ligeramente_saber_la_lengua_arauiga.jpg)](/wiki/File:1506_Arabic_transcription_-_Arte_para_ligeramente_saber_la_lengua_arauiga.jpg)

[De Alcalá](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alcal%C3%A1 "Pedro de Alcalá")'s work has been called the "first Western system of Arabic scientific transcription".[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Early Romanization of the Arabic language was standardized in the various [bilingual Arabic-European dictionaries](/wiki/List_of_Arabic_dictionaries#Bilingual_dictionaries "List of Arabic dictionaries") of the 17–19th centuries:

* [Pedro de Alcalá](/wiki/Pedro_de_Alcal%C3%A1 "Pedro de Alcalá"), *Vocabulista*, 1505. A Spanish-Arabic glossary using a systematic transcription.[[3]](#cite_note-Lipiński-3)
* [Valentin Schindler](/wiki/Valentin_Schindler "Valentin Schindler"), *Lexicon Pentaglotton: Hebraicum, Chaldicum, Syriacum, Talmudico-Rabbinicum, et Arabicum*, 1612. Arabic lemmas were printed in Hebrew characters.[[3]](#cite_note-Lipiński-3)
* [Franciscus Raphelengius](/wiki/Franciscus_Raphelengius "Franciscus Raphelengius"), [*Lexicon Arabicum*](https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_84yAEE271K8C), Leiden 1613. The first printed dictionary of the Arabic language in Arabic characters.[[3]](#cite_note-Lipiński-3)
* [Jacobus Golius](/wiki/Jacobus_Golius "Jacobus Golius"), *Lexicon Arabico-Latinum*, Leiden 1653. The dominant Arabic dictionary in Europe for almost two centuries.[[3]](#cite_note-Lipiński-3)
* [Georg Freytag](/wiki/Georg_Freytag "Georg Freytag"), *Lexicon Arabico-Latinum*, praesertim ex Djeuharii Firuzubadiique et aliorum libris confectum I–IV, Halle 1830–1837[[3]](#cite_note-Lipiński-3)
* [Edward William Lane](/wiki/Edward_William_Lane "Edward William Lane"), *[Arabic–English Lexicon](/wiki/Arabic%E2%80%93English_Lexicon "Arabic–English Lexicon")*, 8 vols, London-Edinburgh 1863–1893. Highly influential, but incomplete (stops at Kaf)[[3]](#cite_note-Lipiński-3)

### Mixed digraphic and diacritical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mixed digraphic and diacritical")]

Further information: [Digraph (orthography)](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") and [Diacritic](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic")

* **[BGN/PCGN](/wiki/BGN/PCGN_romanization "BGN/PCGN romanization")** romanization (1956).[[4]](#cite_note-BGN-PCGN-4)
* **[UNGEGN](/wiki/UNGEGN "UNGEGN")** (1972). United Nations Group of Experts on Geographical Names, or "Variant A of the Amended Beirut System". Adopted from BGN/PCGN.[[5]](#cite_note-UNGEGN-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
  + **[IGN](/wiki/Institut_G%C3%A9ographique_National "Institut Géographique National") System 1973** or "Variant B of the Amended Beirut System", that conforms to the French orthography and is preferred to the Variant A in French-speaking countries as in Maghreb and Lebanon.[[5]](#cite_note-UNGEGN-5)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
  + **ADEGN** romanization (2007) is different from UNGEGN in two ways: (1) ظ is d͟h instead of z̧; (2) the cedilla is replaced by a sub-macron (\_) in all the characters with the cedilla.[[5]](#cite_note-UNGEGN-5)
* **[ALA-LC](/wiki/ALA-LC "ALA-LC")** (first published 1991), from the [American Library Association](/wiki/American_Library_Association "American Library Association") and the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress").[[8]](#cite_note-ALA-LC-8) This romanization is close to the romanization of the *[Deutsche Morgenländische Gesellschaft](/wiki/Deutsche_Morgenl%C3%A4ndische_Gesellschaft "Deutsche Morgenländische Gesellschaft")* and [Hans Wehr](/wiki/Hans_Wehr "Hans Wehr"), which is used internationally in scientific publications by [Arabists](/wiki/Arabist "Arabist").
  + **IJMES**, used by *International Journal of Middle East Studies*, very similar to ALA-LC.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
  + **EI**, *[Encyclopaedia of Islam](/wiki/Encyclopaedia_of_Islam "Encyclopaedia of Islam")* (1st ed., 1913–1938; 2nd ed., 1960–2005).[[10]](#cite_note-EI-10)

### Fully diacritical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fully diacritical")]

* **DMG** (**[Deutsche Morgenländische Gesellschaft](/wiki/Deutsche_Morgenl%C3%A4ndische_Gesellschaft "Deutsche Morgenländische Gesellschaft")**, 1935), adopted by the International Convention of Orientalist Scholars in Rome.[[11]](#cite_note-DGM1935-11)
  + **[DIN 31635](/wiki/DIN_31635 "DIN 31635")** (1982), developed by the German Institute for Standardization (**[Deutsches Institut für Normung](/wiki/Deutsches_Institut_f%C3%BCr_Normung "Deutsches Institut für Normung")**).
  + **[Hans Wehr transliteration](/wiki/Hans_Wehr_transliteration "Hans Wehr transliteration")** (1961, 1994), a modification to DIN 31635.
  + **EALL**, *Encyclopedia of Arabic Language and Linguistics* (edited by Kees Versteegh, Brill, 2006–2009).[[12]](#cite_note-EALL-12)
  + Spanish romanization, identical to DMG/DIN with the exception of three letters: ǧ > ŷ, ḫ > j, ġ > g.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* **[ISO 233](/wiki/ISO_233 "ISO 233")** (1984), letter-to-letter; vowels are transliterated only if they are shown with diacritics, otherwise they are omitted.
  + [ISO 233-2](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_233-2 "fr:ISO 233-2") (1993), simplified transliteration; vowels are always shown.[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
* [BS 4280](/w/index.php?title=BS_4280&action=edit&redlink=1 "BS 4280 (page does not exist)") (1968), developed by the [British Standards Institution](/wiki/BSI_Group "BSI Group").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### ASCII-based

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: ASCII-based")]

* [ArabTeX](/wiki/ArabTeX "ArabTeX") (since 1992) has been modelled closely after the transliteration standards [ISO/R 233](/wiki/ISO/R_233 "ISO/R 233") and DIN 31635.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* [Buckwalter Transliteration](/wiki/Buckwalter_Transliteration "Buckwalter Transliteration") (1990s), developed at ALPNET by [Tim Buckwalter](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Buckwalter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tim Buckwalter (page does not exist)"); does not require [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* [Arabic **chat** alphabet](/wiki/Arabic_chat_alphabet "Arabic chat alphabet"):[[12]](#cite_note-EALL-12) an ad hoc solution for conveniently entering Arabic using a Latin keyboard.

## Romanization issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Romanization issues")]

Any [romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") system has to make a number of decisions which are dependent on its intended field of application.

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Vowels")]

One basic problem is that written Arabic is normally [unvocalized](/wiki/Harakat "Harakat"); i.e., many of the [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") are not written out, and must be supplied by a reader familiar with the language. Hence unvocalized [Arabic writing](/wiki/Abjad "Abjad") does not give a reader unfamiliar with the language sufficient information for accurate pronunciation. As a result, a pure [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration"), e.g., rendering قطر as ***qṭr***, is meaningless to an untrained reader. For this reason, [transcriptions](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") are generally used that add vowels, e.g. ***qaṭar***. However, unvocalized systems match exactly to written Arabic, unlike vocalized systems such as Arabic chat, which some claim detracts from one's ability to spell.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Transliteration vs. transcription

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transliteration vs. transcription")]

Most uses of romanization call for [transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") rather than [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration"): Instead of transliterating each written letter, they try to reproduce the sound of the words according to the orthography rules of the target language: *Qaṭar*. This applies equally to scientific and popular applications. A pure transliteration would need to omit vowels (e.g. *qṭr*), making the result difficult to interpret except for a subset of trained readers fluent in Arabic. Even if vowels are added, a transliteration system would still need to distinguish between multiple ways of spelling the same sound in the Arabic script, e.g. **alif** ا vs. **alif maqṣūrah** ى for the sound /aː/ **ā**, and the six different ways (ء إ أ آ ؤ ئ) of writing the [glottal stop](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop") ([hamza](/wiki/Hamza "Hamza"), usually transcribed **ʼ**). This sort of detail is needlessly confusing, except in a very few situations (e.g., typesetting text in the Arabic script).

Most issues related to the romanization of Arabic are about transliterating vs. transcribing; others, about what should be romanized:

* Some transliterations ignore [assimilation](/wiki/Assimilation_(linguistics) "Assimilation (linguistics)") of the [definite article](/wiki/Definite_article "Definite article") *[al-](/wiki/Al- "Al-")* before the "[sun letters](/wiki/Sun_letters "Sun letters")", and may be easily misread by non-Arabic speakers. For instance, "the light" النور *an-nūr* would be more literally transliterated along the lines of *alnūr*. In the transcription *an-nūr*, a hyphen is added and the unpronounced /[l](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolar lateral approximant")/ removed for the convenience of the uninformed non-Arabic speaker, who would otherwise pronounce an /l/, perhaps not understanding that /n/ in *nūr* is [geminated](/wiki/Gemination "Gemination"). Alternatively, if the [shaddah](/wiki/Shaddah "Shaddah") is not transliterated (since it is strictly not a letter), a strictly literal transliteration would be *alnūr*, which presents similar problems for the uninformed non-Arabic speaker.
* A transliteration should render the "closed tāʼ" (*[tāʼ marbūṭah](/wiki/Ta%27_marbuta "Ta' marbuta")*, ة) faithfully. Many transcriptions render the sound /a/ as *a* or *ah* and *t* when it denotes /at/.
  + [ISO 233](/wiki/ISO_233 "ISO 233") has a unique symbol, ẗ.
* "Restricted alif" (**alif maqṣūrah**, ى) should[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] be transliterated with an [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent"), *á*, differentiating it from regular alif ا, but it is transcribed in many schemes like alif, *ā*, because it stands for /aː/.
* [Nunation](/wiki/Nunation "Nunation"): what is true elsewhere is also true for nunation: transliteration renders what is seen, transcription what is heard, when in the Arabic script, it is written with diacritics, not by letters, or omitted.

A transcription may reflect the language as spoken, typically rendering names, for example, by the people of Baghdad ([Baghdad Arabic](/wiki/Baghdad_Arabic "Baghdad Arabic")), or the official standard ([Literary Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic")) as spoken by a [preacher](/wiki/Imam "Imam") in the [Mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") or a TV newsreader. A transcription is free to add phonological (such as vowels) or morphological (such as word boundaries) information. Transcriptions will also vary depending on the writing conventions of the target language; compare English *[Omar Khayyam](/wiki/Omar_Khayyam "Omar Khayyam")* with German *Omar Chajjam*, both for عمر خيام /ʕumar xajjaːm/, [[ˈʕomɑr xæjˈjæːm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Arabic "Help:IPA/Arabic") (unvocalized **ʿmr ḫyām**, vocalized **ʻUmar Khayyām**).

A transliteration is ideally fully reversible: a machine should be able to transliterate it back into Arabic. A transliteration can be considered as flawed for any one of the following reasons:

* A "loose" transliteration is ambiguous, rendering several Arabic phonemes with an identical transliteration, or such that digraphs for a single phoneme (such as *dh gh kh sh th* rather than *ḏ ġ ḫ š ṯ*) may be confused with two adjacent consonants—but this problem is resolved in the [ALA-LC](/wiki/ALA-LC "ALA-LC") romanization system, where the [prime symbol](/wiki/Prime_(symbol) "Prime (symbol)") ʹ is used to separate two consonants when they do not form a digraph;[[22]](#cite_note-22) for example: أَكْرَمَتْها **akramatʹhā** ('she honored her'), in which the *t* and *h* are two distinct consonantal sounds, or where the [middle dot](/wiki/Interpunct "Interpunct") is used in the same way in the BGN/PCGN romanization.
* Symbols representing phonemes may be considered too similar (e.g., ʻ and ' or ʿ and ʾ for ع [ʻayn](/wiki/Ayin "Ayin") and [hamzah](/wiki/Hamzah "Hamzah"));
* ASCII transliterations using capital letters to disambiguate phonemes[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] are easy to type, but may be considered unaesthetic.

A fully accurate transcription may not be necessary for native Arabic speakers, as they would be able to pronounce names and sentences correctly anyway, but it can be very useful for those not fully familiar with spoken Arabic and who are familiar with the Roman alphabet. An accurate transliteration serves as a valuable stepping stone for learning, pronouncing correctly, and distinguishing phonemes. It is a useful tool for anyone who is familiar with the sounds of Arabic but not fully conversant in the language.

One criticism is that a fully accurate system would require special learning that most do not have to actually pronounce names correctly, and that with a lack of a universal romanization system they will not be pronounced correctly by non-native speakers anyway. The precision will be lost if special characters are not replicated and if a reader is not familiar with Arabic pronunciation.

## Arabic alphabet and nationalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Arabic alphabet and nationalism")]

There have been many instances of national movements to convert [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_alphabet "Arabic alphabet") into Latin script or to romanize the language.

### Lebanon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Lebanon")]

See also: [Lebanese Arabic](/wiki/Lebanese_Arabic "Lebanese Arabic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/f/fe/Lebnaan_Newspaper_issue_686.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lebnaan_Newspaper_issue_686.jpg)

*LEBNAAN* in proposed [Said Akl](/wiki/Said_Akl "Said Akl") alphabet (issue #686)

A Beirut newspaper, *La Syrie*, pushed for the change from Arabic script to [Latin script](/wiki/Lebanese_Arabic#Said_Akl's_orthography "Lebanese Arabic") in 1922. The major head of this movement was [Louis Massignon](/wiki/Louis_Massignon "Louis Massignon"), a French Orientalist, who brought his concern before the Arabic Language Academy in Damascus in 1928. Massignon's attempt at romanization failed as the Academy and the population viewed the proposal as an attempt from the Western world to take over their country. Sa'id Afghani, a member of the Academy, asserted that the movement to romanize the script was a Zionist plan to dominate Lebanon.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24)

### Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Arabic&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Egypt")]

After the period of colonialism in Egypt, Egyptians were looking for a way to reclaim and reemphasize Egyptian culture. As a result, some Egyptians pushed for an Egyptianization of the [Arabic language](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") in which the formal Arabic and the [colloquial Arabic](/wiki/Egyptian_Arabic "Egyptian Arabic") would be combined into one language and the Latin alphabet would be used.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24) There was also the idea of finding a way to use [hieroglyphics](/wiki/Hieroglyphics "Hieroglyphics") instead of the Latin alphabet.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24) A scholar, [Salama Musa](/wiki/Salama_Musa "Salama Musa"), agreed with the idea of applying a Latin alphabet to Egyptian Arabic, as he believed that would allow Egypt to have a closer relationship with the West. He also believed that Latin script was key to the success of Egypt as it would allow for more advances in [science](/wiki/Science "Science") and [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology"). This change in script, he believed, would solve the problems inherent with Arabic, such as a lack of written vowels and difficulties writing foreign words.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Ahmad Lutfi As Sayid](/wiki/Ahmed_Lutfi_el-Sayed "Ahmed Lutfi el-Sayed") and [Muhammad Azmi](/wiki/Muhammad_Mustafa_Azmi "Muhammad Mustafa Azmi"), two Egyptian intellectuals, agreed with Musa and supported the push for romanization.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24) The idea that romanization was necessary for modernization and growth in Egypt continued with Abd Al Aziz Fahmi in 1944. He was the chairman for the Writing and Grammar Committee for the Arabic Language Academy of Cairo.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24) He believed and desired to implement romanization in a way that allowed words and spellings to remain somewhat familiar to the Egyptian people. However, this effort failed as the Egyptian people felt a strong cultural tie to the Arabic alphabet, particularly the older generation.[[23]](#cite_note-shrivtiel179-23)[[24]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-24)
